Narrower than Geology (the whole solid Earth): it focuses on the individual crystalline substances that compose rocks, distinct from Volcanology and Seismology, which study dynamic processes.
nodes.cs: Studium minerálů — jejich chemického složení, krystalové struktury, fyzikálních vlastností, vzniku a klasifikace. Užší než geologie (celá pevná Země): zaměřuje se na jednotlivé krystalické látky tvořící horniny, odlišné od vulkanologie a seismologie, jež studují dynamické procesy.
nodes.brief:
Mineralogy is the study of minerals — their chemical composition, crystal structure, physical properties, formation, and classification. It focuses on the individual crystalline substances that compose rocks, one level narrower than the whole-Earth materials and processes that geology addresses.

Within [[01.04]], mineralogy supplies [[01.04.01]] with the compositional building blocks of the rocks geology maps and classifies, and it studies the crystals that [[01.04.07]] watches form as magma cools — the two fields meet wherever molten rock solidifies into mineral matter. Mineralogy's chemical foundations also connect it across branches to [[01.02]], whose principles of bonding, structure, and composition it applies to naturally occurring crystalline solids.

For further reading, see the Mineralogical Society of America's [educational resources](https://www.minsocam.org/msa/collectors_corner/) and the [Encyclopaedia Britannica entry on mineralogy](https://www.britannica.com/science/mineralogy).

nodes.name: Crystallography
nodes.en: Studies the ordered internal atomic arrangement of crystals and its expression as external symmetry, providing the geometric foundation for identifying minerals.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá uspořádané vnitřní atomové uspořádání krystalů a jeho projev v podobě vnější symetrie a poskytuje geometrický základ pro určování minerálů.

nodes.name: Crystal systems & symmetry
nodes.en: The seven crystal systems into which all crystalline solids fall, defined by their axial relationships and the symmetry operations they possess.
nodes.cs: Sedm krystalových soustav, do nichž spadají všechny krystalické látky, vymezených vztahy os a operacemi symetrie, jimiž disponují.
nodes.brief:
The seven crystal systems into which all crystalline solids fall, defined by their axial relationships and the symmetry operations they possess.

### Concepts
- **Crystal system** — One of seven categories—such as cubic or hexagonal—grouping crystals by their axial geometry.
- **Symmetry elements** — The rotation axes, mirror planes, and inversion centers that describe a crystal's internal order.
- **Miller indices** — A notation of three integers specifying the orientation of a crystal face or plane.

nodes.name: Crystal lattice & defects
nodes.en: The periodic three-dimensional array of atoms defining a crystal, and the point, line, and planar imperfections that govern many physical properties.
nodes.cs: Periodické trojrozměrné uspořádání atomů vymezující krystal a bodové, čárové a plošné poruchy, jež řídí mnohé fyzikální vlastnosti.
nodes.brief:
The periodic three-dimensional array of atoms defining a crystal, and the point, line, and planar imperfections that govern many physical properties.

### Concepts
- **Unit cell** — The smallest repeating volume that, translated in three dimensions, builds the entire crystal lattice.
- **Lattice defect** — An irregularity such as a vacancy or dislocation that disrupts the perfect atomic array and alters behavior.

nodes.name: Crystal Chemistry
nodes.en: Relates a mineral's composition and bonding to its atomic structure, explaining how ionic size and charge dictate which structures form and substitute.
nodes.cs: Vztahuje složení a vazby minerálu k jeho atomové stavbě a vysvětluje, jak velikost a náboj iontů určují, které struktury vznikají a zastupují se.

nodes.name: Silicate structures
nodes.en: The classification of the dominant rock-forming minerals by how their silica tetrahedra link, from isolated to fully polymerized frameworks.
nodes.cs: Klasifikace převládajících horninotvorných minerálů podle způsobu propojení jejich křemíkových čtyřstěnů, od izolovaných po plně polymerizované skelety.
nodes.brief:
The classification of the dominant rock-forming minerals by how their silica tetrahedra link, from isolated to fully polymerized frameworks.

### Concepts
- **Silica tetrahedron** — The fundamental building block of a silicon atom bonded to four oxygen atoms, the unit of all silicate minerals.
- **Polymerization degree** — The extent to which silica tetrahedra share oxygen atoms, distinguishing silicate structural classes.

nodes.name: Isomorphism & solid solution
nodes.en: The substitution of ions of similar size and charge within a fixed structure, producing continuous compositional series such as the plagioclase feldspars.
nodes.cs: Nahrazování iontů podobné velikosti a náboje v pevné struktuře, jež vytváří spojité kompoziční řady jako plagioklasové živce.
nodes.brief:
The substitution of ions of similar size and charge within a fixed structure, producing continuous compositional series such as the plagioclase feldspars.

### Concepts
- **Solid solution** — A mineral series in which one ion progressively replaces another without changing the crystal structure.
- **Coupled substitution** — The paired exchange of ions that together preserve overall electrical neutrality.

nodes.name: Physical & Optical Mineralogy
nodes.en: Examines the observable and optical properties by which minerals are identified, linking each property to underlying crystal structure and bonding.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá pozorovatelné a optické vlastnosti, podle nichž se minerály určují, a spojuje každou vlastnost s podkladovou krystalovou stavbou a vazbou.

nodes.name: Physical properties
nodes.en: The diagnostic characteristics—hardness, cleavage, luster, streak, and density—that permit rapid hand-specimen identification of minerals.
nodes.cs: Diagnostické znaky – tvrdost, štěpnost, lesk, vryp a hustota – jež umožňují rychlé určení minerálů podle ručních vzorků.
nodes.brief:
The diagnostic characteristics—hardness, cleavage, luster, streak, and density—that permit rapid hand-specimen identification of minerals.

### Concepts
- **Mohs hardness scale** — A relative ranking of mineral scratch resistance from talc at one to diamond at ten.
- **Cleavage** — The tendency of a mineral to break along planes of structural weakness into flat surfaces.
- **Luster** — The way a mineral surface reflects light, described as metallic, vitreous, or earthy among others.

nodes.name: Optical mineralogy
nodes.en: The identification of minerals from their interaction with polarized light under the microscope, exploiting refraction, birefringence, and interference colors.
nodes.cs: Určování minerálů podle jejich interakce s polarizovaným světlem pod mikroskopem, jež využívá lom, dvojlom a interferenční barvy.
nodes.brief:
The identification of minerals from their interaction with polarized light under the microscope, exploiting refraction, birefringence, and interference colors.

### Concepts
- **Birefringence** — The splitting of light into two rays with different velocities in an anisotropic crystal.
- **Extinction** — The position at which a crystal appears dark between crossed polarizers, diagnostic of its orientation and identity.

nodes.name: Systematic Mineralogy & Genesis
nodes.en: Classifies minerals by chemical composition into major groups and explains the geological environments in which each characteristically forms.
nodes.cs: Třídí minerály podle chemického složení do hlavních skupin a vysvětluje geologická prostředí, v nichž se každá charakteristicky tvoří.

nodes.name: Mineral classification
nodes.en: The chemical taxonomy of minerals into classes—silicates, oxides, sulfides, carbonates, sulfates, and others—by their dominant anion group.
nodes.cs: Chemická taxonomie minerálů do tříd – silikáty, oxidy, sulfidy, uhličitany, sírany a další – podle jejich převládající aniontové skupiny.
nodes.brief:
The chemical taxonomy of minerals into classes—silicates, oxides, sulfides, carbonates, sulfates, and others—by their dominant anion group.

### Concepts
- **Mineral class** — A grouping such as sulfides or carbonates defined by the principal anion or anionic complex.
- **Native elements** — Minerals composed of a single uncombined element, such as gold, copper, or diamond.

nodes.name: Mineral formation environments
nodes.en: The distinct settings—magmatic, hydrothermal, metamorphic, and sedimentary—that produce characteristic mineral assemblages by their pressure, temperature, and fluids.
nodes.cs: Odlišná prostředí – magmatická, hydrotermální, metamorfní a sedimentární – jež svým tlakem, teplotou a fluidy vytvářejí charakteristická minerální společenstva.
nodes.brief:
The distinct settings—magmatic, hydrothermal, metamorphic, and sedimentary—that produce characteristic mineral assemblages by their pressure, temperature, and fluids.

### Concepts
- **Hydrothermal mineral** — A mineral precipitated from hot aqueous fluids circulating through rock fractures.
- **Evaporite mineral** — A mineral such as halite or gypsum formed by the evaporation of saline water.

nodes.en: The study of volcanoes and volcanic phenomena — magma, eruptions, lava, volcanic gases, and the hazards and landforms they create. A process-focused Earth-science subfield distinct from Seismology (earthquakes) and Mineralogy (mineral matter): its subject is the movement of molten rock to and at the surface.
nodes.cs: Studium sopek a vulkanických jevů — magmatu, erupcí, lávy, sopečných plynů a nebezpečí a tvarů reliéfu, jež vytvářejí. Podobor věd o Zemi zaměřený na procesy, odlišný od seismologie (zemětřesení) a mineralogie (minerální hmota): jeho předmětem je pohyb roztavené horniny k povrchu a na něm.
nodes.brief:
Volcanology is the study of volcanoes and volcanic phenomena — magma generation and ascent, eruption dynamics, lava, volcanic gases, and the hazards and landforms they produce. It combines field monitoring, geochemistry, and geophysics to understand how molten rock moves from Earth's interior to its surface and what happens when it erupts.

Within [[01.04]], volcanology traces the same molten rock whose eventual products [[01.04.06]] classifies once it cools into mineral matter, and it is frequently monitored alongside [[01.04.08]], since the fracturing and fluid movement that precede an eruption generate the seismic signals seismology detects. Its process-focused view of the solid Earth complements the broader structural and historical scope of [[01.04.01]].

For further orientation, see the Smithsonian's [Global Volcanism Program](https://volcano.si.edu/) and the USGS [Volcano Hazards Program](https://www.usgs.gov/programs/VHP).

nodes.name: Magma & Igneous Processes
nodes.en: Studies how molten rock is generated in the mantle and crust and how its composition and physical properties determine the character of the volcanism it feeds.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá, jak vzniká roztavená hornina v plášti a kůře a jak její složení a fyzikální vlastnosti určují povahu vulkanismu, který živí.

nodes.name: Magma generation
nodes.en: The partial melting of rock through decompression, addition of volatiles, or heating that produces magma at spreading ridges, subduction zones, and hotspots.
nodes.cs: Částečné tavení horniny dekompresí, přidáním těkavých látek nebo zahřátím, jež vytváří magma na riftových hřbetech, v subdukčních zónách a nad horkými skvrnami.
nodes.brief:
The partial melting of rock through decompression, addition of volatiles, or heating that produces magma at spreading ridges, subduction zones, and hotspots.

### Concepts
- **Decompression melting** — The formation of magma when rising mantle rock melts as pressure decreases without added heat.
- **Flux melting** — The lowering of a rock's melting point by water and volatiles released from a subducting plate.
- **Hotspot** — A persistent volcanic center fed by a deep mantle plume, largely independent of plate boundaries.

nodes.name: Magma composition & rheology
nodes.en: The silica and volatile content controlling magma viscosity and gas retention, which together dictate whether an eruption is gentle or violently explosive.
nodes.cs: Obsah oxidu křemičitého a těkavých látek řídící viskozitu magmatu a zadržování plynů, jež společně určují, zda je erupce mírná, nebo prudce explozivní.
nodes.brief:
The silica and volatile content controlling magma viscosity and gas retention, which together dictate whether an eruption is gentle or violently explosive.

### Concepts
- **Viscosity** — A magma's resistance to flow, increasing with silica content and controlling eruption style.
- **Volatile content** — The dissolved gases such as water and carbon dioxide whose exsolution drives explosive eruptions.
- **Magma types** — The compositional spectrum from fluid basaltic to viscous rhyolitic magma.

nodes.name: Eruption Dynamics
nodes.en: Analyzes the physical mechanisms by which magma reaches and exits the surface, spanning the continuum from quiet lava effusion to catastrophic explosive fragmentation.
nodes.cs: Analyzuje fyzikální mechanismy, jimiž magma dosahuje povrchu a vystupuje z něj, v celém rozpětí od klidného výlevu lávy po katastrofickou explozivní fragmentaci.

nodes.name: Effusive eruptions
nodes.en: The relatively gentle outpouring of low-viscosity lava that flows across the surface to build shields and lava plateaus.
nodes.cs: Poměrně mírný výron nízkoviskózní lávy, jež proudí po povrchu a buduje štítové sopky a lávové plošiny.
nodes.brief:
The relatively gentle outpouring of low-viscosity lava that flows across the surface to build shields and lava plateaus.

### Concepts
- **Lava flow** — A stream of molten rock advancing across the surface, forming pahoehoe or aa textures as it cools.
- **Fissure eruption** — The emission of lava along an elongated crack rather than a central vent.

nodes.name: Explosive eruptions
nodes.en: The violent fragmentation of viscous gas-rich magma that ejects ash and pumice into towering columns and generates lethal pyroclastic flows.
nodes.cs: Prudká fragmentace viskózního plynem bohatého magmatu, jež vyvrhuje popel a pemzu do vysokých sloupců a vytváří smrtící pyroklastické proudy.
nodes.brief:
The violent fragmentation of viscous gas-rich magma that ejects ash and pumice into towering columns and generates lethal pyroclastic flows.

### Concepts
- **Pyroclastic flow** — A fast, ground-hugging avalanche of hot gas and volcanic debris, among the deadliest volcanic hazards.
- **Plinian eruption** — A sustained explosive eruption producing a high stratospheric column of gas and ash.
- **Volcanic Explosivity Index** — A logarithmic scale rating eruption magnitude by erupted volume and column height.

nodes.name: Volcanic products
nodes.en: The solid, liquid, and gaseous materials expelled during eruptions, from lava and tephra to volcanic gases that influence climate.
nodes.cs: Pevné, kapalné a plynné materiály vyvržené při erupcích, od lávy a tefry po vulkanické plyny ovlivňující klima.
nodes.brief:
The solid, liquid, and gaseous materials expelled during eruptions, from lava and tephra to volcanic gases that influence climate.

### Concepts
- **Tephra** — Fragmental material of all sizes ejected explosively and transported through the air.
- **Volcanic gas** — Emissions such as water vapor, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide released from magma.

nodes.name: Volcanic Landforms
nodes.en: Classifies the edifices and structures built and destroyed by volcanic activity, whose shape records the eruption style and magma type that formed them.
nodes.cs: Třídí tělesa a struktury budované a ničené sopečnou činností, jejichž tvar zaznamenává styl erupce a typ magmatu, který je vytvořil.

nodes.name: Volcano types
nodes.en: The principal volcanic edifices—shield, stratovolcano, and cinder cone—distinguished by their profile, size, and the eruptions that construct them.
nodes.cs: Hlavní sopečná tělesa – štítová sopka, stratovulkán a struskový kužel – rozlišená svým profilem, velikostí a erupcemi, jež je budují.
nodes.brief:
The principal volcanic edifices—shield, stratovolcano, and cinder cone—distinguished by their profile, size, and the eruptions that construct them.

### Concepts
- **Shield volcano** — A broad gently sloped volcano built by fluid basaltic lava flows.
- **Stratovolcano** — A steep-sided composite cone of alternating lava and pyroclastic layers, prone to explosive eruptions.
- **Cinder cone** — A small steep hill built from ejected fragments accumulating around a single vent.

nodes.name: Calderas & craters
nodes.en: The depressions formed by collapse of a partly emptied magma chamber or by explosive excavation, marking the largest volcanic events.
nodes.cs: Sníženiny vzniklé zborcením částečně vyprázdněné magmatické komory nebo explozivním vyhloubením, jež značí největší sopečné události.
nodes.brief:
The depressions formed by collapse of a partly emptied magma chamber or by explosive excavation, marking the largest volcanic events.

### Concepts
- **Caldera** — A large basin formed when the roof of an emptied magma chamber collapses after a major eruption.
- **Supervolcano** — A volcanic system capable of an eruption exceeding a thousand cubic kilometers of material.

nodes.name: Volcanic Hazards & Monitoring
nodes.en: Assesses the threats volcanoes pose to people and the geophysical surveillance techniques used to detect unrest and forecast eruptions.
nodes.cs: Hodnotí hrozby, jež sopky představují pro lidi, a geofyzikální metody sledování používané k rozpoznání neklidu a předpovědi erupcí.

nodes.name: Volcanic hazards
nodes.en: The direct and indirect dangers of eruptions, including pyroclastic flows, lahars, ashfall, and gas emissions that endanger life and disrupt climate and aviation.
nodes.cs: Přímá a nepřímá nebezpečí erupcí, včetně pyroklastických proudů, laharů, spadu popela a emisí plynů, jež ohrožují život a narušují klima a letectví.
nodes.brief:
The direct and indirect dangers of eruptions, including pyroclastic flows, lahars, ashfall, and gas emissions that endanger life and disrupt climate and aviation.

### Concepts
- **Lahar** — A fast-moving mudflow of volcanic debris and water descending a volcano's flanks.
- **Ashfall** — The settling of airborne volcanic ash that collapses roofs, damages engines, and harms health.

nodes.name: Volcano monitoring
nodes.en: The continuous measurement of seismicity, ground deformation, and gas emission that signals magma movement and underpins eruption forecasting.
nodes.cs: Nepřetržité měření seismicity, deformace terénu a emisí plynů, jež signalizuje pohyb magmatu a je základem předpovědi erupcí.
nodes.brief:
The continuous measurement of seismicity, ground deformation, and gas emission that signals magma movement and underpins eruption forecasting.

### Concepts
- **Volcanic tremor** — Continuous seismic vibration caused by moving magma and fluids, often preceding eruption.
- **Ground deformation** — The swelling or subsidence of a volcano's surface as magma accumulates or drains, tracked by GPS and radar.

nodes.en: The study of seismic waves and earthquakes — their generation, propagation through the Earth, measurement, and what they reveal about the planet's interior structure. Distinct from Volcanology (molten rock) and Geology (materials): it centers on elastic-wave energy and ground motion.
nodes.cs: Studium seismických vln a zemětřesení — jejich vzniku, šíření Zemí, měření a toho, co odhalují o vnitřní stavbě planety. Odlišná od vulkanologie (roztavená hornina) a geologie (materiály): soustředí se na energii pružných vln a pohyby půdy.
nodes.brief:
Seismology is the study of seismic waves and earthquakes — their generation, propagation through the Earth, measurement, and what they reveal about the planet's interior structure. It uses networks of sensitive instruments to locate earthquakes, characterize fault rupture, and image layers deep beneath the crust that no drill can reach.

Within [[01.04]], seismology shares its quantitative core with [[01.01]], applying physical laws of wave propagation to Earth's interior, and it is closely tied to [[01.04.07]], whose eruptions are frequently preceded by the seismic signals seismology monitors. Its wave-based imaging complements [[01.04.01]]'s surface-based reading of structure, together building a fuller picture of the solid Earth from crust to core.

For further reading, see the IRIS/EarthScope [Seismic Monitor](https://www.iris.edu/hq/) and the USGS [Earthquake Hazards Program](https://earthquake.usgs.gov/).

nodes.name: Seismic Waves
nodes.en: Studies the elastic waves radiated by earthquakes and their propagation through the Earth, whose behavior encodes both the source and the medium they traverse.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá elastické vlny vyzářené zemětřeseními a jejich šíření Zemí, jejichž chování v sobě nese informaci o zdroji i o prostředí, jímž procházejí.

nodes.name: Body waves (P & S)
nodes.en: The compressional and shear waves that travel through the Earth's interior, whose differing speeds and paths reveal deep structure and time the earthquake.
nodes.cs: Podélné a příčné vlny procházející nitrem Země, jejichž odlišné rychlosti a dráhy odhalují hlubinnou stavbu a určují čas zemětřesení.
nodes.brief:
The compressional and shear waves that travel through the Earth's interior, whose differing speeds and paths reveal deep structure and time the earthquake.

### Concepts
- **P-wave** — The fastest seismic wave, a compressional wave that travels through solids and liquids.
- **S-wave** — A slower shear wave that travels only through solids, its absence revealing the liquid outer core.
- **Travel-time curve** — The relation between wave arrival time and distance used to locate earthquakes and probe structure.

nodes.name: Surface waves
nodes.en: The waves confined near the Earth's surface that travel slower than body waves but carry the largest amplitudes, causing most earthquake damage.
nodes.cs: Vlny omezené blízko zemského povrchu, jež se šíří pomaleji než prostorové vlny, ale nesou největší amplitudy a způsobují většinu škod při zemětřesení.
nodes.brief:
The waves confined near the Earth's surface that travel slower than body waves but carry the largest amplitudes, causing most earthquake damage.

### Concepts
- **Love wave** — A horizontally polarized shear surface wave that produces side-to-side ground motion.
- **Rayleigh wave** — A surface wave producing a rolling elliptical ground motion, responsible for much shaking damage.

nodes.name: Earthquake Source Physics
nodes.en: Explains how stress accumulates and releases on faults to generate earthquakes, and how their size, location, and rupture geometry are quantified.
nodes.cs: Vysvětluje, jak se na zlomech hromadí a uvolňuje napětí, čímž vznikají zemětřesení, a jak se určuje jejich velikost, poloha a geometrie porušení.

nodes.name: Fault rupture & elastic rebound
nodes.en: The model in which slowly stored elastic strain in rock is suddenly released as the rock snaps along a fault, radiating seismic waves.
nodes.cs: Model, v němž se pomalu nahromaděné elastické napětí v hornině náhle uvolní, když hornina praskne podél zlomu a vyzáří seismické vlny.
nodes.brief:
The model in which slowly stored elastic strain in rock is suddenly released as the rock snaps along a fault, radiating seismic waves.

### Concepts
- **Elastic rebound theory** — The concept that rocks bend under accumulating stress and rebound sharply when they fracture, causing an earthquake.
- **Hypocenter & epicenter** — The point of rupture at depth and the point on the surface directly above it.
- **Aftershocks** — The sequence of smaller earthquakes following a main shock as the crust readjusts.

nodes.name: Magnitude & intensity
nodes.en: The distinct measures of an earthquake's released energy and of the shaking experienced at a location, quantified by the moment magnitude and intensity scales.
nodes.cs: Odlišné míry uvolněné energie zemětřesení a otřesů pociťovaných na daném místě, vyjádřené momentovou magnitudou a stupnicí intenzity.
nodes.brief:
The distinct measures of an earthquake's released energy and of the shaking experienced at a location, quantified by the moment magnitude and intensity scales.

### Concepts
- **Moment magnitude** — A logarithmic measure of the total energy released, based on fault area, slip, and rock rigidity.
- **Mercalli intensity** — A scale rating the observed effects and damage of shaking at a particular place.

nodes.name: Focal mechanisms
nodes.en: The determination of fault orientation and slip direction from the pattern of seismic wave first motions, revealing the tectonic stress that drove the quake.
nodes.cs: Určení orientace zlomu a směru posunu z rozložení prvních pohybů seismických vln, jež odhaluje tektonické napětí, které zemětřesení vyvolalo.
nodes.brief:
The determination of fault orientation and slip direction from the pattern of seismic wave first motions, revealing the tectonic stress that drove the quake.

### Concepts
- **Beachball diagram** — The graphical representation of a focal mechanism showing the fault type and orientation.
- **Fault type solution** — The identification of normal, reverse, or strike-slip motion from the radiated wave pattern.

nodes.name: Earth Structure from Seismology
nodes.en: Uses the propagation of seismic waves as a probe of the planet's inaccessible interior, resolving its layered structure and lateral heterogeneity.
nodes.cs: Využívá šíření seismických vln jako sondu do nedostupného nitra planety a rozlišuje jeho vrstevnatou stavbu a boční nehomogenitu.

nodes.name: Internal structure
nodes.en: The division of the Earth into crust, mantle, and core, inferred from the refraction, reflection, and shadow zones of seismic waves.
nodes.cs: Rozdělení Země na kůru, plášť a jádro odvozené z lomu, odrazu a stínových zón seismických vln.
nodes.brief:
The division of the Earth into crust, mantle, and core, inferred from the refraction, reflection, and shadow zones of seismic waves.

### Concepts
- **Seismic discontinuity** — A depth where wave velocity changes abruptly, marking a boundary such as the Moho or core-mantle boundary.
- **Shadow zone** — A range of surface distances where certain waves fail to arrive, revealing the liquid outer core.

nodes.name: Seismic tomography
nodes.en: The three-dimensional imaging of the Earth's interior from the travel times of many crossing seismic rays, mapping hot upwellings and cold sinking slabs.
nodes.cs: Trojrozměrné zobrazování nitra Země z dob doběhu mnoha křížících se seismických paprsků, jež mapuje horké výstupy a chladné klesající desky.
nodes.brief:
The three-dimensional imaging of the Earth's interior from the travel times of many crossing seismic rays, mapping hot upwellings and cold sinking slabs.

### Concepts
- **Velocity anomaly** — A region where wave speed deviates from average, indicating temperature or compositional differences.
- **Slab imaging** — The detection of cold subducted plates as fast-velocity zones descending into the mantle.

nodes.name: Seismic Hazard & Engineering
nodes.en: Translates seismological knowledge into estimates of earthquake risk and into warning and design measures that reduce loss of life and property.
nodes.cs: Převádí seismologické poznatky do odhadů zemětřesného rizika a do varovných a projektových opatření, jež snižují ztráty na životech a majetku.

nodes.name: Seismic hazard assessment
nodes.en: The probabilistic estimation of the ground shaking a site may experience, combining fault activity, recurrence, and local soil amplification.
nodes.cs: Pravděpodobnostní odhad otřesů, jimž může být místo vystaveno, jenž spojuje aktivitu zlomů, opakování a místní zesílení půdou.
nodes.brief:
The probabilistic estimation of the ground shaking a site may experience, combining fault activity, recurrence, and local soil amplification.

### Concepts
- **Ground motion prediction** — An empirical relation estimating shaking intensity as a function of magnitude and distance.
- **Site amplification** — The intensification of shaking where soft sediments magnify seismic waves.

nodes.name: Earthquake early warning
nodes.en: Systems that detect the fast, weak P-wave and issue alerts seconds before the destructive S and surface waves arrive.
nodes.cs: Systémy, jež zachytí rychlou slabou P-vlnu a vydají výstrahu vteřiny před příchodem ničivých S-vln a povrchových vln.
nodes.brief:
Systems that detect the fast, weak P-wave and issue alerts seconds before the destructive S and surface waves arrive.

### Concepts
- **P-wave detection** — The rapid recognition of the first-arriving compressional wave to trigger a warning.
- **Warning lead time** — The interval between alert and strong shaking, allowing automatic protective actions.

nodes.en: The study of water on and beneath the land surface — its distribution, movement, and quality through the water cycle, including rivers, groundwater, and watersheds. Distinct from Oceanography (the seas) and Meteorology (atmospheric water): its domain is terrestrial fresh water and its circulation.
nodes.cs: Studium vody na povrchu souše a pod ním — jejího rozložení, pohybu a jakosti v koloběhu vody, včetně řek, podzemní vody a povodí. Odlišná od oceánografie (moře) a meteorologie (atmosférická voda): jejím doménou je pevninská sladká voda a její oběh.
nodes.brief:
Hydrology is the study of water on and beneath the land surface — its distribution, movement, and quality through the water cycle, including rivers, groundwater, and watersheds. It quantifies how precipitation becomes runoff, infiltration, and streamflow, and it underpins water-resource management, flood forecasting, and drought assessment.

Within [[01.04]], hydrology picks up the water cycle where [[01.04.02]] leaves off — meteorology delivers the precipitation that hydrology then tracks across the land surface — and it hands that water on to [[01.04.03]] once rivers reach the sea. It also shares its subject with [[01.04.10]], which studies water's frozen form: glaciers and ice sheets act as slow-release reservoirs within the same hydrological cycle hydrology quantifies in its liquid phase.

For further orientation, see the USGS [Water Science School](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topics/water-science-school) and UNESCO's [International Hydrological Programme](https://www.unesco.org/en/ihp).

nodes.name: Hydrological Cycle
nodes.en: Describes the continuous circulation of water among ocean, atmosphere, and land through evaporation, precipitation, and runoff that governs freshwater availability.
nodes.cs: Popisuje nepřetržitý oběh vody mezi oceánem, atmosférou a pevninou prostřednictvím výparu, srážek a odtoku, jenž řídí dostupnost sladké vody.

nodes.name: Precipitation & evapotranspiration
nodes.en: The atmospheric input of water to land and its return by evaporation and plant transpiration, the two dominant fluxes of the terrestrial water balance.
nodes.cs: Atmosférický přísun vody na pevninu a její návrat výparem a transpirací rostlin, dva převládající toky pevninské vodní bilance.
nodes.brief:
The atmospheric input of water to land and its return by evaporation and plant transpiration, the two dominant fluxes of the terrestrial water balance.

### Concepts
- **Evapotranspiration** — The combined loss of water to the atmosphere from soil evaporation and plant transpiration.
- **Interception** — The precipitation captured by vegetation surfaces before it reaches the ground.

nodes.name: Water balance
nodes.en: The accounting of inflows, outflows, and storage change for a region or basin, expressing conservation of water over a defined period.
nodes.cs: Bilancování přítoků, odtoků a změny zásoby pro oblast nebo povodí, jež vyjadřuje zachování vody za vymezené období.
nodes.brief:
The accounting of inflows, outflows, and storage change for a region or basin, expressing conservation of water over a defined period.

### Concepts
- **Water budget equation** — The balance stating that precipitation equals evapotranspiration plus runoff plus change in storage.
- **Residence time** — The average time a water molecule spends in a reservoir such as a lake or aquifer.

nodes.name: Surface Water Hydrology
nodes.en: Studies water flowing and stored on the land surface, from rainfall-generated runoff through river networks to lakes, quantifying streamflow and flood behavior.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá vodu proudící a uloženou na zemském povrchu, od odtoku vzniklého ze srážek přes říční sítě po jezera, a kvantifikuje průtok a chování povodní.

nodes.name: Runoff & streamflow
nodes.en: The generation of surface flow when rainfall exceeds infiltration, and its concentration into channels whose discharge integrates the response of a catchment.
nodes.cs: Vznik povrchového odtoku, když srážky převýší vsak, a jeho soustředění do koryt, jejichž průtok shrnuje odezvu povodí.
nodes.brief:
The generation of surface flow when rainfall exceeds infiltration, and its concentration into channels whose discharge integrates the response of a catchment.

### Concepts
- **Discharge** — The volume of water passing a river cross-section per unit time.
- **Hydrograph** — A plot of streamflow over time showing a catchment's response to a rainfall event.
- **Infiltration** — The entry of water into the soil, whose capacity determines how much rainfall becomes runoff.

nodes.name: Rivers & drainage basins
nodes.en: The organization of surface water into channel networks draining defined catchments, whose geometry controls flow routing and sediment transport.
nodes.cs: Uspořádání povrchové vody do říčních sítí odvodňujících vymezená povodí, jejichž geometrie řídí postup průtoku a transport sedimentu.
nodes.brief:
The organization of surface water into channel networks draining defined catchments, whose geometry controls flow routing and sediment transport.

### Concepts
- **Watershed divide** — The topographic boundary separating adjacent drainage basins.
- **Stream order** — A hierarchical numbering of channels reflecting the branching structure of a river network.

nodes.name: Floods
nodes.en: The overtopping of channels when discharge exceeds capacity, characterized statistically by recurrence intervals for hazard assessment and design.
nodes.cs: Vybřežení koryt, když průtok překročí kapacitu, statisticky charakterizované dobami opakování pro hodnocení rizika a navrhování.
nodes.brief:
The overtopping of channels when discharge exceeds capacity, characterized statistically by recurrence intervals for hazard assessment and design.

### Concepts
- **Return period** — The average interval between floods of a given magnitude, used to define design events.
- **Flash flood** — A rapid inundation from intense rainfall over a short time, often in steep or urban catchments.

nodes.name: Groundwater Hydrology
nodes.en: Studies water stored and moving beneath the surface within porous rock and sediment, the largest accessible reservoir of unfrozen freshwater.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá vodu uloženou a pohybující se pod povrchem v propustných horninách a sedimentech – největší dostupnou zásobárnu nezamrzlé sladké vody.

nodes.name: Aquifers
nodes.en: The subsurface bodies of permeable rock or sediment that store and transmit usable quantities of groundwater, classified by confinement and recharge.
nodes.cs: Podpovrchová tělesa propustných hornin nebo sedimentů, jež zadržují a předávají využitelné množství podzemní vody, tříděná podle napjatosti a doplňování.
nodes.brief:
The subsurface bodies of permeable rock or sediment that store and transmit usable quantities of groundwater, classified by confinement and recharge.

### Concepts
- **Water table** — The upper surface of the saturated zone where pore pressure equals atmospheric pressure.
- **Confined aquifer** — A water-bearing layer bounded above and below by impermeable beds, often under pressure.
- **Porosity & permeability** — The fraction of void space in a material and its capacity to transmit fluid through connected pores.

nodes.name: Groundwater flow
nodes.en: The slow movement of water through porous media driven by hydraulic gradients, governed quantitatively by Darcy's law.
nodes.cs: Pomalý pohyb vody porézním prostředím hnaný hydraulickými gradienty, kvantitativně popsaný Darcyho zákonem.
nodes.brief:
The slow movement of water through porous media driven by hydraulic gradients, governed quantitatively by Darcy's law.

### Concepts
- **Darcy's law** — The relation that groundwater discharge is proportional to hydraulic conductivity and the hydraulic gradient.
- **Hydraulic head** — The energy of groundwater per unit weight, whose spatial gradient drives flow.

nodes.name: Water Quality & Hydrochemistry
nodes.en: Examines the chemical and biological constituents of natural waters and the processes that transport and transform contaminants through hydrological systems.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá chemické a biologické složky přírodních vod a procesy, jež přenášejí a přeměňují znečišťující látky hydrologickými systémy.

nodes.name: Water quality parameters
nodes.en: The physical, chemical, and biological indicators—pH, dissolved oxygen, nutrients, and pathogens—that define water's suitability for use and ecosystems.
nodes.cs: Fyzikální, chemické a biologické ukazatele – pH, rozpuštěný kyslík, živiny a patogeny – jež vymezují vhodnost vody pro užití a ekosystémy.
nodes.brief:
The physical, chemical, and biological indicators—pH, dissolved oxygen, nutrients, and pathogens—that define water's suitability for use and ecosystems.

### Concepts
- **Dissolved oxygen** — The amount of oxygen present in water, essential for aquatic life and an index of pollution.
- **Eutrophication** — The over-enrichment of water with nutrients that triggers algal blooms and oxygen depletion.

nodes.name: Contaminant transport
nodes.en: The movement of dissolved and suspended pollutants through surface and groundwater by advection, dispersion, and reaction.
nodes.cs: Pohyb rozpuštěných a suspendovaných znečišťujících látek povrchovou a podzemní vodou advekcí, disperzí a reakcí.
nodes.brief:
The movement of dissolved and suspended pollutants through surface and groundwater by advection, dispersion, and reaction.

### Concepts
- **Advection-dispersion** — The combined bulk transport and spreading of a solute carried by flowing water.
- **Contaminant plume** — The spreading zone of pollution migrating away from a source through an aquifer.

nodes.name: Applied Hydrology & Water Resources
nodes.en: Applies hydrological understanding to predict water availability and manage its allocation, storage, and protection for human and ecological needs.
nodes.cs: Uplatňuje hydrologické poznatky k předpovědi dostupnosti vody a k řízení jejího rozdělování, uchovávání a ochrany pro lidské a ekologické potřeby.

nodes.name: Hydrological modeling
nodes.en: The mathematical simulation of catchment processes to forecast streamflow, floods, and water balance under varying climate and land use.
nodes.cs: Matematická simulace procesů v povodí k předpovědi průtoku, povodní a vodní bilance za měnícího se klimatu a využívání půdy.
nodes.brief:
The mathematical simulation of catchment processes to forecast streamflow, floods, and water balance under varying climate and land use.

### Concepts
- **Rainfall-runoff model** — A model transforming precipitation input into predicted streamflow at a catchment outlet.
- **Model calibration** — The adjustment of model parameters so simulated flows match observed records.

nodes.name: Water resource management
nodes.en: The planning and governance of water supply, storage, and demand across sectors to balance competing uses and sustain aquatic ecosystems.
nodes.cs: Plánování a správa zásobování vodou, jejího uchovávání a poptávky napříč odvětvími k vyvážení konkurenčních užití a udržení vodních ekosystémů.
nodes.brief:
The planning and governance of water supply, storage, and demand across sectors to balance competing uses and sustain aquatic ecosystems.

### Concepts
- **Integrated water resources management** — A coordinated approach balancing social, economic, and ecological water demands within a basin.
- **Environmental flow** — The water regime maintained in a river to sustain its ecosystems and services.

nodes.en: The study of ice in the natural environment — glaciers, ice sheets, ice caps, and their formation, movement, and role in the climate system and sea level. A cryosphere-focused Earth-science subfield distinct from Hydrology's liquid-water domain and Climatology's atmospheric focus.
nodes.cs: Studium ledu v přírodním prostředí — ledovců, ledovcových štítů, ledových čepic a jejich vzniku, pohybu a role v klimatickém systému a výšce hladiny moří. Podobor věd o Zemi zaměřený na kryosféru, odlišný od hydrologie (kapalná voda) a klimatologie (atmosféra).
nodes.brief:
Glaciology is the study of ice in the natural environment — glaciers, ice sheets, and ice caps, their formation, movement, and role in the climate system and sea-level change. It combines field measurement, remote sensing, and ice-core drilling to track how frozen water accumulates, flows, and melts.

Within [[01.04]], glaciology supplies [[01.04.04]] with some of its longest direct records: ice cores preserve trapped air and layered snowfall that reconstruct past temperature and atmospheric composition far beyond the instrumental era. It occupies the frozen end of the same water cycle [[01.04.09]] tracks in its liquid phase, and it shares a deep-time, physical-record approach with [[01.04.05]], whose fossils and glaciology's ice cores both preserve Earth's history for later reading.

For further reading, see the National Snow and Ice Data Center's [glaciers primer](https://nsidc.org/learn/parts-cryosphere/glaciers) and NASA's [Ice sheets resource](https://climate.nasa.gov/vital-signs/ice-sheets/).

nodes.name: Glacier Formation & Mass Balance
nodes.en: Studies how snow transforms into glacier ice and how the yearly gain and loss of mass determine whether a glacier advances, retreats, or holds steady.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá, jak se sníh mění v ledovcový led a jak roční přírůstek a úbytek hmoty určují, zda ledovec postupuje, ustupuje, nebo stagnuje.

nodes.name: Snow to ice transformation
nodes.en: The progressive densification of accumulated snow through firn into solid glacier ice as burial expels air and welds crystals.
nodes.cs: Postupné zhušťování nahromaděného sněhu přes firn na pevný ledovcový led, jak pohřbení vytlačuje vzduch a spéká krystaly.
nodes.brief:
The progressive densification of accumulated snow through firn into solid glacier ice as burial expels air and welds crystals.

### Concepts
- **Firn** — Partially compacted granular snow that has survived a melt season on its way to becoming glacier ice.
- **Densification** — The compaction of snow and firn under its own weight into denser ice over years to centuries.

nodes.name: Mass balance & equilibrium line
nodes.en: The net difference between accumulation and ablation over a year, whose sign controls a glacier's growth and whose boundary is the equilibrium line.
nodes.cs: Čistý rozdíl mezi akumulací a ablací za rok, jehož znaménko řídí růst ledovce a jehož hranicí je rovnovážná čára.
nodes.brief:
The net difference between accumulation and ablation over a year, whose sign controls a glacier's growth and whose boundary is the equilibrium line.

### Concepts
- **Accumulation & ablation** — The gain of mass by snowfall and its loss by melting, sublimation, and calving.
- **Equilibrium line altitude** — The elevation where annual accumulation exactly balances ablation, a sensitive climate indicator.

nodes.name: Glacier Dynamics
nodes.en: Explains how glacier ice deforms and slides under gravity, the mechanisms controlling flow velocity, and the unstable behaviors of surging and calving.
nodes.cs: Vysvětluje, jak se ledovcový led deformuje a klouže pod tíhou, mechanismy řídící rychlost toku a nestabilní chování při ledovcových výdutích a odlamování.

nodes.name: Ice flow & deformation
nodes.en: The movement of glaciers by internal creep of ice crystals and by basal sliding, whose combination sets the flow rate and surface features.
nodes.cs: Pohyb ledovců vnitřním tečením ledových krystalů a klouzáním po podloží, jejichž kombinace určuje rychlost toku a povrchové tvary.
nodes.brief:
The movement of glaciers by internal creep of ice crystals and by basal sliding, whose combination sets the flow rate and surface features.

### Concepts
- **Basal sliding** — The movement of a glacier over its bed, lubricated by meltwater at the ice-rock interface.
- **Internal deformation** — The slow flow of ice by creep of its crystals under gravitational stress.
- **Crevasse** — A deep open fracture formed where a glacier flows over uneven terrain and the brittle surface ice cracks.

nodes.name: Glacial surging & calving
nodes.en: The episodic rapid advance of surge-type glaciers and the breaking off of ice at tidewater and lake margins, dominant loss processes for many ice masses.
nodes.cs: Občasný rychlý postup výdutových ledovců a odlamování ledu na mořských a jezerních okrajích – převládající procesy úbytku mnoha ledových hmot.
nodes.brief:
The episodic rapid advance of surge-type glaciers and the breaking off of ice at tidewater and lake margins, dominant loss processes for many ice masses.

### Concepts
- **Glacier surge** — A sudden brief period of greatly accelerated flow followed by a long quiescent phase.
- **Calving** — The breaking away of ice blocks from a glacier terminus into water, forming icebergs.

nodes.name: Glacial Geomorphology
nodes.en: Studies the erosional and depositional landforms created by glaciers, which record the extent and behavior of past and present ice masses.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá erozní a akumulační tvary vytvořené ledovci, jež zaznamenávají rozsah a chování dřívějších i současných ledových hmot.

nodes.name: Glacial erosion
nodes.en: The sculpting of bedrock by ice through plucking and abrasion, carving cirques, U-shaped valleys, and fjords that mark glaciated terrain.
nodes.cs: Opracování skalního podloží ledem vylamováním a obrusem, jež vytesává kary, údolí tvaru U a fjordy, které značí zaledněnou krajinu.
nodes.brief:
The sculpting of bedrock by ice through plucking and abrasion, carving cirques, U-shaped valleys, and fjords that mark glaciated terrain.

### Concepts
- **Cirque** — A bowl-shaped hollow eroded at the head of a glacier where it originates.
- **U-shaped valley** — A steep-sided glacially carved valley, contrasting with the V-shape of river valleys.
- **Striations** — Scratches gouged into bedrock by rock fragments dragged along the glacier base, recording ice flow direction.

nodes.name: Glacial deposition
nodes.en: The laying down of transported debris as ice melts, forming moraines, till, and outwash that map former ice positions and meltwater flow.
nodes.cs: Ukládání transportovaného materiálu při tání ledu, jež vytváří morény, till a fluvioglaciální sedimenty mapující dřívější polohy ledu a odtok tavné vody.
nodes.brief:
The laying down of transported debris as ice melts, forming moraines, till, and outwash that map former ice positions and meltwater flow.

### Concepts
- **Moraine** — A ridge or mound of unsorted debris deposited directly by a glacier at its margins or terminus.
- **Till** — The unsorted, unstratified sediment deposited directly by glacier ice.
- **Outwash** — Stratified sediment carried and sorted by meltwater streams flowing from a glacier.

nodes.name: Ice Sheets & Cryosphere
nodes.en: Examines the continental ice sheets and the wider frozen realm of permafrost and sea ice, central to global sea level and climate feedbacks.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá kontinentální ledovcové štíty a širší zmrzlou říši permafrostu a mořského ledu, jež jsou klíčové pro globální hladinu moře a klimatické zpětné vazby.

nodes.name: Ice sheets & sea level
nodes.en: The vast Antarctic and Greenland ice masses whose growth and melt drive the largest changes in global sea level over glacial cycles and today.
nodes.cs: Rozsáhlé antarktické a grónské ledové hmoty, jejichž růst a tání pohánějí největší změny globální hladiny moře během ledovcových cyklů i v současnosti.
nodes.brief:
The vast Antarctic and Greenland ice masses whose growth and melt drive the largest changes in global sea level over glacial cycles and today.

### Concepts
- **Ice sheet** — A continental-scale mass of glacial ice exceeding fifty thousand square kilometers, as on Antarctica and Greenland.
- **Ice shelf** — A floating extension of an ice sheet over the ocean that buttresses the flow of ice behind it.
- **Sea-level contribution** — The rise in ocean level produced when land ice melts and adds water to the sea.

nodes.name: Permafrost & sea ice
nodes.en: The perennially frozen ground of high latitudes and the seasonally forming sea ice, both sensitive climate indicators and carbon-cycle actors.
nodes.cs: Trvale zmrzlá půda vysokých zeměpisných šířek a sezonně vznikající mořský led, oba citlivé klimatické ukazatele a činitelé uhlíkového cyklu.
nodes.brief:
The perennially frozen ground of high latitudes and the seasonally forming sea ice, both sensitive climate indicators and carbon-cycle actors.

### Concepts
- **Permafrost** — Ground that remains at or below freezing for at least two consecutive years, storing vast amounts of carbon.
- **Sea ice** — Frozen seawater that forms and melts seasonally, strongly influencing polar albedo and ocean circulation.
